explanation: Correct answer: C — styrene is NOT a common component of smoke from industrial fires. Carbon monoxide (A), particulate matter (B), and formaldehyde (D) are all common combustion products released when organic materials burn. Styrene is primarily associated with plastics/rubber manufacturing emissions rather than being a common component of general industrial fire smoke. [Source: 2000Q Ch33 Occupational Toxicology; Casarett & Doull Ch.34]

Correct answer: A — Collecting air samples in evacuated containers carries the risk that reactive or unstable analytes can degrade during storage, especially if the container is not properly passivated or if collection and analysis are separated in time. Temperature differences (B) and incomplete removal of heavier gases (C) are also potential issues, but A captures the primary limitation. The DB answer (A) is consistent with the original docx answer key. [Source: 2000Q Ch33 Occupational Toxicology; NIOSH Manual of Analytical Methods]

Correct answer: C — calcium carbonate is NOT a medium for collecting gases and vapors for environmental testing. Charcoal (A), silica gel (B), and gas chromatography column-packing material (D) are all established sorbent media used for trapping volatile organic compounds and gases from air samples. Calcium carbonate is not used as a gas/vapor collection sorbent. [Source: 2000Q Ch33 Occupational Toxicology; NIOSH Manual of Analytical Methods]

Correct answer: D — A color-changing badge (or passive dosimeter badge) is a useful device for detecting the presence of a gas or vapor above a threshold toxic level without requiring specialized training. These badges change color upon exposure to specific chemicals and provide a simple visual indication of hazard. Gas chromatographs (A) and liquid chromatographs (B) require trained operators. [Source: 2000Q Ch33 Occupational Toxicology]

Correct match: chlorine → water treatment workers (letter F per answer key). Note: The DB correctly stores the letter (F) but incorrectly stores the option text as 'microelectronics industry' (which is actually the match for arsine). Chlorine is widely used as a disinfectant in municipal water treatment, making this the correct occupational association. [Source: 2000Q Ch33 Occupational Toxicology answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.34]

Correct match: phosgene → pesticide manufacturing, welding (letter N per answer key). Note: The DB correctly stores the letter (N) but incorrectly stores the option text as 'furniture manufacturers' (which was the display-line pairing). Phosgene (COCl₂) is a highly toxic gas used as an intermediate in pesticide and polyurethane production and is also produced by thermal decomposition of chlorinated solvents during welding. [Source: 2000Q Ch33 Occupational Toxicology answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.24, 34]

Correct match: ozone → arc welders and printers (letter G per answer key). Note: The DB correctly stores the letter (G) but incorrectly stores the option text as 'glass etching' (which was the display-line pairing). Ozone (O₃) is generated by ultraviolet radiation from arc welding and by corona discharge in photocopiers and laser printers, making these the key occupational exposure sources. [Source: 2000Q Ch33 Occupational Toxicology answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.34]

Correct match: ethylene oxide → gas sterilizing systems (letter E per answer key). Note: The DB correctly stores the letter (E) but incorrectly stores the option text as 'beer and wine fermentation' (which was the display-line pairing). Ethylene oxide (EtO) is a potent alkylating agent used primarily for low-temperature gas sterilization of medical equipment and spices — it is NOT involved in fermentation. [Source: 2000Q Ch33 Occupational Toxicology answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.34]

Correct match: formaldehyde → furniture manufacturers (letter B per answer key). Note: The DB correctly stores the letter (B) but incorrectly stores the option text as 'gas sterilizing systems' (which is actually the match for ethylene oxide). Formaldehyde is a key component of urea-formaldehyde and phenol-formaldehyde resins used in particle board, MDF, and other pressed-wood furniture. [Source: 2000Q Ch33 Occupational Toxicology answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.24, 34]

Correct match: nitrogen dioxide → agricultural workers (letter M per answer key). Note: The DB correctly stores the letter (M) but incorrectly stores the option text as 'water treatment workers' (which was the display-line pairing). NO₂ is generated from silage in silos (silo filler's disease), making agricultural workers the primary occupationally exposed group. [Source: 2000Q Ch33 Occupational Toxicology answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.15, 34]

Correct match: arsine → microelectronics industry (letter A per answer key). Note: The DB correctly stores the letter (A) but incorrectly stores the option text as 'simple asphyxiant in coal mines' (which is the match for methane). Arsine (AsH₃) is used as a dopant gas in semiconductor manufacturing — it is a potent hemolytic poison, NOT a simple asphyxiant. [Source: 2000Q Ch33 Occupational Toxicology answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.23, 34]

Correct match: methyl bromide → fumigant applicators (letter O). Methyl bromide (CH₃Br) is a broad-spectrum fumigant used for soil fumigation, structural pest control, and commodity treatment. Due to its ozone-depleting properties, it is being phased out under the Montreal Protocol. Exposure primarily affects fumigation workers. The DB correctly stores both the letter and the match. [Source: 2000Q Ch33 Occupational Toxicology answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.34]

Correct match: chloroacetophenone → tear gas (letter K). Chloroacetophenone (CN) is a lachrymatory agent used as a riot control agent (tear gas). It is a potent irritant of the eyes, respiratory tract, and skin, causing intense tearing and blepharospasm. It is classified as a sternutator. The DB correctly stores both the letter and the match. [Source: 2000Q Ch33 Occupational Toxicology answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.34]

Correct match: zinc chloride → smoke bombs (letter Q). Zinc chloride (ZnCl₂) is used in military and civilian smoke screens and smoke bombs. It produces a dense white smoke by reacting with moisture in the air, forming zinc oxychloride particles. Inhalation of zinc chloride smoke can cause chemical pneumonitis. The DB correctly stores both the letter and the match. [Source: 2000Q Ch33 Occupational Toxicology answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.34]

Correct match: hydrogen sulfide → sewage and manure workers (letter J). H₂S is produced by bacterial decomposition of organic matter in the absence of oxygen. It is a potent inhibitor of cytochrome c oxidase (like cyanide), causing rapid respiratory arrest at high concentrations. Sewage treatment workers, manure handlers, and oil/gas workers are at highest risk. The DB correctly stores both the letter and the match. [Source: 2000Q Ch33 Occupational Toxicology answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.34]

Correct match: methane → simple asphyxiant in coal mines (letter H). Methane (CH₄), known as firedamp in coal mining, is a colorless, odorless gas that displaces oxygen in enclosed spaces. It acts as a simple asphyxiant — it causes hypoxia by reducing the partial pressure of oxygen in inspired air, not by a direct toxic mechanism. Coal mine explosions from methane accumulation are also a major hazard. The DB correctly stores both the letter and the match. [Source: 2000Q Ch33 Occupational Toxicology answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.34]

Correct match: argon → inert gas simple asphyxiant (letter I). Argon is a noble gas that is chemically inert. In enclosed spaces, it can displace oxygen and act as a simple asphyxiant. It is heavier than air, so it accumulates in low-lying areas. Argon is used as a shielding gas in welding, where this asphyxiation risk is most relevant. The DB correctly stores both the letter and the match. [Source: 2000Q Ch33 Occupational Toxicology answer key]

Correct match: hydrofluoric acid → glass etching (letter C). Hydrofluoric acid (HF) is used in the glass etching industry because it dissolves silica (SiO₂ → SiF₄). HF is a highly corrosive acid that also causes systemic toxicity via fluoride ion chelation of calcium and magnesium (hypocalcemia, hypomagnesemia, dysrhythmias), distinguishing it from other mineral acids. The DB correctly stores both the letter and the match. [Source: 2000Q Ch33 Occupational Toxicology answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.34]

Correct answer per original docx key: D — highly lipid-soluble molecules. Note: The DB stores B (organic cations) but the Ch5 ADE answer key and standard toxicology both support D. After glomerular filtration, only highly lipid-soluble (non-ionized, lipophilic) molecules are passively reabsorbed across the tubular epithelium. Ionized or polar molecules (organic anions, cations, neutral polar molecules) are trapped in the tubular fluid and excreted in urine. This is the principle of ion trapping in renal excretion. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5]

Correct answer per original docx key: A — organic acids. Note: The DB stores B (organic bases) but the answer key and standard toxicology support A. A high (alkaline) urinary pH favors excretion of weak acids (organic acids) because they become ionized (deprotonated) at alkaline pH and are trapped in the tubular lumen, unable to diffuse back across the membrane. Conversely, weak bases become unionized at high pH and are reabsorbed. This is the ion trapping principle used in urinary alkalinization therapy for salicylate poisoning. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5, Ch.7]

Correct answer per original docx key: B — compounds that are ion trapped in the tubular lumen. Note: The DB stores C (highly lipid soluble) but the answer key supports B. Diuretics increase urine flow, which enhances elimination of compounds already ionized (ion-trapped) in the tubular lumen — these compounds are polar and cannot be reabsorbed, so their elimination is flow-dependent. Highly lipid-soluble compounds (C) would simply be reabsorbed and not benefit from increased urine flow. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5]

Correct answer: B — directly proportional to its vapor pressure. The amount of a volatile chemical excreted by the lungs follows Henry's Law: pulmonary excretion rate is directly proportional to the vapor pressure (volatility) and inversely proportional to blood solubility. Higher vapor pressure means more chemical partitions into alveolar air and is exhaled. This is why ethanol (high vapor pressure) is readily detected in breath, whereas less volatile compounds are primarily renally excreted. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5, Ch.7]

Correct answer per original docx key: C — displacement of bilirubin from plasma proteins. Note: The DB stores A (enzyme induction leading to decreased glucocorticoid levels) but the answer key and standard toxicology support C. Kernicterus is bilirubin-induced neurological damage in neonates. Drugs like sulfonamides displace bilirubin from albumin binding sites, increasing free (unconjugated) bilirubin in plasma, which crosses the immature blood-brain barrier and deposits in the basal ganglia. This is a classic protein-binding displacement interaction. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5]

Correct answer per original docx key: D — time of day. Note: The DB stores C (presence of food) but the answer key supports D. GI absorption of xenobiotics IS influenced by pH (A), intestinal microflora (B), and the presence of food (C — affects transit time, gastric emptying, solubility). Time of day (D) is not an independent determinant — circadian variations in absorption are mediated through the other listed factors. The 'except' answer is therefore D. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5]

Correct answer per original docx key: A — hepatic blood flow. Note: The DB stores E (no E option exists — this is a parsing error) but the answer key and standard physiology support A. Per Fick's law of diffusion, the rate of GI diffusion is proportional to surface area (B), permeability (C), and residence time (D). Hepatic blood flow (A) determines first-pass hepatic metabolism after absorption but does NOT influence the initial rate of diffusion across the GI epithelium. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5, Ch.7]

Correct answer per original docx key: C — glucose. Note: The DB stores A (water) but the answer key and physiology support C. The colon actively absorbs water (A), sodium (B), and hydrogen ions (D) — water conservation is a key colonic function. Glucose is NOT absorbed in the colon; it is absorbed exclusively in the small intestine via SGLT1 transporters. The absence of glucose transport in the colon is why glucose-based oral rehydration solutions are ineffective when delivered rectally. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5]

Correct answer: D — 0.1 µm (100 nm). Nanoparticles are defined as having at least one dimension smaller than 100 nm (0.1 µm). Particles between 0.1–10 µm are considered fine particles, while those below 0.1 µm exhibit unique physicochemical properties (high surface-to-volume ratio, quantum effects, enhanced cellular uptake) that distinguish them as nanoparticles. This size threshold is used by regulatory and research organizations worldwide. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.29]

Correct answer: C — 'Because of turbulence, very few [nanoparticles] reach the alveoli' is the FALSE statement (the 'except' answer). Nanoparticles are so small (<0.1 µm) that they evade the major deposition mechanisms (impaction, sedimentation) and are preferentially deposited in the alveoli by diffusion. They are capable of exposing the lung to a large number of particles (A) and a large particle surface area (B), and are the focus of extensive toxicologic research (D). [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5, Ch.29]

Correct answer per original docx key: B — diquat. Note: The DB stores E (no E option — parsing error) but the answer key supports B. Lead (A), strontium (C), and fluoride (D) are all significantly stored in bone matrix: lead substitutes for calcium in hydroxyapatite, strontium follows calcium pathways, and fluoride is incorporated into bone as fluorapatite. Diquat (a bipyridyl herbicide) does NOT accumulate in bone — it is predominantly excreted unchanged in urine. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5, Ch.23]

Correct answer per original docx key: A — heparin. Note: The DB stores D (IGG antibody) but the answer key and immunology support A. IgG antibodies cross the placenta via FcRn-mediated transport (crucial for neonatal passive immunity), rubella virus (B) and spirochetes (C) are known to cross the placenta and cause congenital infections. Heparin (A) is a large, highly sulfated, negatively charged polysaccharide that does NOT cross the placenta — it is the anticoagulant of choice in pregnancy for this reason. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5, Ch.21]

Correct answer per original docx key: D — methionine. Note: The DB stores B (glutamine) but the answer key and toxicology support D. Methylmercury (MeHg) forms a complex with cysteine (MeHg-S-Cys) that structurally mimics the amino acid methionine. This mimic allows MeHg-cysteine to be transported across the blood-brain barrier by the large neutral amino acid (LNAA) transporter (system L). This molecular mimicry mechanism explains why MeHg is the most neurotoxic form of mercury — it hijacks an essential amino acid transporter to gain CNS access. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5, Ch.23]

Correct answer per original docx key: A — 'Acidic compounds may be more concentrated in milk than plasma' is the FALSE statement. Note: The DB stores C but the answer key supports A. Breast milk has a slightly lower pH (≈7.0) than plasma (≈7.4). Weak BASES become ion-trapped in the more acidic milk, making them MORE concentrated in milk than plasma — not weak acids. Option C (toxicants passed from cows to humans through milk) IS true — examples include melamine and aflatoxin M1. DDT, PCBs, and PBBs (D) are well-documented persistent organic pollutants found in human milk. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5]

Correct answer: C — nonsaturability (the exception). Active transport is characterized by movement against a concentration gradient (A), energy requirement (B), saturability (limited carrier capacity), and competitive inhibition (D). Nonsaturability (C) is the opposite — active transport IS saturable because carrier proteins have finite capacity. Simple diffusion, by contrast, is nonsaturable. This distinguishes carrier-mediated from passive transport. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5]

Correct answer per original docx key: B — movement against a concentration gradient (the exception for facilitated diffusion). Note: The DB stores C (saturability) but the answer key supports B. Facilitated diffusion: does NOT require energy (A), moves ALONG (not against) a concentration gradient (B is FALSE → exception), IS saturable because of carrier-mediated transport (C is TRUE), and involves a carrier protein (D). Saturability is a defining feature of facilitated diffusion — the DB's answer is incorrect. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5]

Correct answer per original docx key: A — phagocytosis. Note: The DB stores D (mediated through MDR1/P-gp) but the answer key supports A. Alveolar macrophages remove inhaled particles (including insoluble particulates, bacteria, and debris) from the alveolar surface through phagocytosis — engulfing particles into phagolysosomes for degradation. MDR1/P-gp (D) is an efflux transporter for small molecules/drugs at the cell membrane, NOT a mechanism for removing particulate matter from alveoli. Phospholipidosis (B) is a pathological accumulation of phospholipids, not a transport process. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5, Ch.15]

Correct answer per original docx key: D — efflux transporters. Note: The DB stores C (metal transporters) but the answer key supports D. P-gp (ABCB1), MRP2 (ABCC2), and BCRP (ABCG2) on enterocyte brush-border membranes are ATP-dependent efflux transporters that pump xenobiotics BACK into the intestinal lumen, limiting oral bioavailability. They are part of the ATP-binding cassette (ABC) transporter superfamily and function as a detoxification barrier, not as metal transporters or influx transporters. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5]

Correct answer per original docx key: C — ethanol (the exception). Note: The DB stores D (colchicine) but the answer key supports C. P-gp substrates include cyclosporin A (A — immunosuppressant), paclitaxel (B — anticancer drug), and colchicine (D — anti-gout agent). Ethanol (C) is NOT a P-gp substrate — it is a small, highly diffusible molecule that crosses membranes by passive diffusion and is metabolized by alcohol dehydrogenase and CYP2E1. The DB incorrectly identifies colchicine as the exception when it IS a known P-gp substrate. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5]

Correct answer per original docx key: C — Fick's law. Note: The DB stores B (Priestley's law) but the answer key and standard biophysics support C. Fick's law of diffusion states that the rate of transfer by simple diffusion is proportional to the concentration gradient, surface area, and membrane permeability, and inversely proportional to membrane thickness. Priestley discovered oxygen — there is no 'Priestley's law' relevant to this context. Dalton's law (A) governs partial pressures in gas mixtures. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5]

Correct answer per original docx key: D — unsaturated fatty acids. Note: The DB stores C (ion channels) but the answer key supports D. Cell membrane fluidity is determined by the ratio of unsaturated to saturated fatty acids in the phospholipid bilayer. Unsaturated fatty acids have double bonds that create kinks in the lipid tails, preventing tight packing and increasing fluidity. Saturated fatty acids (A) pack tightly and decrease fluidity. Ion channels (C) and transmembrane proteins (B) are embedded in the membrane but do not determine its fluid character. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5]

Correct answer per original docx key: C — small hydrophilic molecules. Note: The DB stores E (no E option — parsing error) but the answer key supports C. Aqueous pores (water-filled channels in cell membranes) allow passage of small water-soluble (hydrophilic) molecules and ions. Small hydrophobic molecules (A) pass directly through the lipid bilayer by dissolving in it, not through pores. Large molecules (B, D) cannot fit through aqueous pores regardless of polarity. The pore size limit is approximately 0.4 nm for paracellular transport. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5]

Correct answer per original docx key: D — TCDD. Note: The DB stores C (acetic acid) but the answer key and chemistry support D. TCDD (2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin) has an octanol/water partition coefficient (log Kow) of approximately 6.8, making it extremely lipophilic. Paraquat (A, log Kow ≈ -4.5) is very hydrophilic, ethylene glycol (B, log Kow ≈ -1.4) is highly water-soluble, and acetic acid (C, log Kow ≈ -0.2) is polar. TCDD's extreme lipophilicity explains its bioaccumulation and persistence in adipose tissue. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5]

Correct answer: A — 60 kDa. The glomerular filtration barrier (endothelial fenestrae, basement membrane, podocyte slit diaphragms) allows passage of molecules up to approximately 60-70 kDa in molecular weight. Molecules smaller than 60 kDa are freely filtered unless they are protein-bound or negatively charged (charge selectivity from the basement membrane heparan sulfate). Molecules larger than 60 kDa are restricted from glomerular filtration. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5, Ch.14]

Correct answer per original docx key: C — active transport. Note: The DB stores E (no E option — parsing error) but the answer key supports C. Most vital nutrients essential for fetal development (glucose, amino acids, vitamins, minerals) are delivered across the placenta by active transport or facilitated diffusion — these processes concentrate nutrients in the fetal circulation. Simple diffusion (A) and ion trapping (D) are the primary mechanisms for most toxicants, but essential nutrients require energy-dependent carrier-mediated transport to achieve the necessary concentration gradients for fetal growth. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5, Ch.21]

Correct answer per original docx key: A — simple diffusion. Note: The DB stores E (no E option — parsing error) but the answer key supports A. Most toxicants cross the placenta by simple diffusion, driven by the concentration gradient between maternal and fetal circulations. This applies to lipophilic, non-ionized xenobiotics of low to moderate molecular weight. Active transport (C) is reserved for endogenous nutrients, while facilitated diffusion (B) and ion trapping (D) are less common mechanisms for toxicant transfer. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5, Ch.21]

Correct answer per original docx key: A — tight endothelial cell junctions similar to the blood-brain barrier (the exception). Note: The DB stores C but the answer key supports A. The placenta protects the fetus through: multiple tissue layers (B — syncytiotrophoblast, cytotrophoblast, fetal endothelium), biotransformation enzymes (C — CYP450, GSTs, and other Phase I/II enzymes), and efflux transporters (D — P-gp, BCRP). However, the placenta does NOT have tight endothelial cell junctions comparable to the BBB — its barrier function comes from the cellular layers, not from uniquely tight paracellular junctions. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5, Ch.21]

Correct answer per original docx key: C — 'They do not require energy from ATP' (the exception). Note: The DB stores D but the correct answer is C. P-gp, MRP2, MRP4, and BCRP are ABC (ATP-Binding Cassette) transporters that DO require ATP hydrolysis for their efflux function — that is their defining biochemical feature. They are located on the luminal (blood-facing) side of brain capillary endothelial cells (A), can efflux uncharged molecules (B), and can handle both anionic (MRPs) and cationic (P-gp) substrates (D). The false statement is C. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5]

Correct answer: B — 'It is remarkably constant throughout all areas of the brain' (the exception). The blood-brain barrier is NOT uniform — it varies significantly across brain regions. Circumventricular organs (area postrema, median eminence, subfornical organ, etc.) lack a functional BBB to allow neuroendocrine sensing. A is true (BBB is not fully developed at birth, though some debate exists), C is true (lipid-soluble chemicals penetrate faster), and D is true (ionized chemicals penetrate slower due to reduced membrane permeability). [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5]

Correct answer: A — large particle size. Elemental mercury (quicksilver) is poorly absorbed orally (<0.01% of an ingested dose) because it coalesces into large droplets in the GI tract, greatly reducing the surface area available for absorption. Unlike ionic mercury compounds (e.g., HgCl₂) that are well-absorbed, the metallic form is not ionized and does not dissolve in aqueous GI fluids, preventing efficient mucosal uptake. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5, Ch.23]

Correct answer per original docx key: C — methemoglobinemia. Note: The DB stores B (toxic megacolon) but the answer key supports C. The higher pH (less acidic) of the infant GI tract alters the gut microbiome and favors the growth of nitrate-reducing bacteria. These bacteria reduce dietary nitrates to nitrites, which are absorbed and oxidize hemoglobin to methemoglobin, impairing oxygen transport. This 'infant methemoglobinemia' is also known as 'blue baby syndrome' and is exacerbated by well water with high nitrate content. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.5, Ch.27]

Correct answer: D — all of the above. Grapefruit juice inhibits intestinal CYP3A4 (A), inhibits intestinal P-glycoprotein (B), and increases the oral bioavailability of drugs like lovastatin (C) that are CYP3A4/P-gp substrates. This food-drug interaction is mediated by furanocoumarins (primarily bergamottin and 6',7'-dihydroxybergamottin) found in grapefruit juice, which cause irreversible inhibition of intestinal CYP3A4, leading to markedly increased systemic drug levels and risk of adverse effects. [Source: 2000Q Ch5 ADE answer key; Casarett & Doull Ch.1, Ch.5]
